tldr: Moving from Deepwall's managed paywall service to OpenIAP means dropping a hosted paywall product for an open cross-platform IAP specification you implement yourself via a conforming library.
why_switch: Eliminate vendor lock-in by adopting a neutral open IAP specification
Remove an extra third-party SDK from the mobile binary
Avoid Deepwall subscription fees in favor of an open spec and OSS implementations
Standardize purchase flows across iOS and Android via a single typed interface
Own the stack end-to-end if your team prefers to avoid managed paywall services
what_to_export: Product IDs and SKUs configured in App Store Connect and Google Play Console
Subscription group definitions and offer codes
A/B test segment assignments and historical results
Purchase event history and analytics configuration
Trigger rules currently driving paywall presentation
Receipt validation logic or webhook handlers
export_method: Deepwall keeps paywall configuration and A/B tests in its dashboard, so manually export what you can from the dashboard and document remote-config rules. Product IDs themselves live in App Store Connect and Google Play Console — those stay in place. Capture historical purchase and experiment analytics through Deepwall's available export options or via your own analytics if you piped events there. Consult Deepwall's official documentation for the current export workflow.
import_method: OpenIAP is a specification, not a service, so you adopt it by integrating an OpenIAP-conforming library for your platform (for example flutter_inapp_purchase or react-native-iap, depending on stack). Wire the library to your existing App Store Connect and Google Play product IDs, then build the paywall UI yourself or with another component. Implement receipt validation server-side or via a separate service. Refer to the OpenIAP spec and the chosen library's docs for current API surface.

concept_mapping.note: OpenIAP doesn't render paywalls — you ship your own UI
Bring an experimentation SDK or remote config separately
Implement trigger conditions in app code or a feature-flag service
Library handles platform billing APIs under a unified interface
OpenIAP is client-side; receipt validation stays your responsibility
Pipe purchase events from the OpenIAP library into your analytics of choice

effort: 1-3 weeks plus paywall UI rebuild time
gotchas: OpenIAP is a spec — you still need to choose and integrate a conforming library
Paywall UI, A/B testing, and analytics are not provided; budget engineering for each
Server-side receipt validation must be implemented or handled by another service
Spec is still maturing per its own documentation, so pin library versions carefully
Historical Deepwall experiment results may not be exportable in a structured form
Run both stacks in parallel for a release cycle to verify purchase parity

email.subject: Proposal: move IAP off Deepwall to OpenIAP-based stack
email.body:
Hi team,

I'm proposing we replace Deepwall with an OpenIAP-conforming library and our own paywall UI. This drops an SDK and a subscription fee, and aligns us with an open spec.

OpenIAP itself is just a spec, so we'd pick a conforming library for our stack. We'll need to rebuild the paywall UI, bring our own A/B testing, and handle receipt validation server-side.

Product IDs in App Store Connect and Google Play stay unchanged. Estimating 1-3 weeks plus paywall UI work. Flagging that some Deepwall experiment history may not export cleanly.

Ready to scope further when you are.
